coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of magnetically separable and reusable Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites photocatalyst utilizing Moringa oleifera extract and watermelon peel for rapid dye degradation

coredata.dc:description: This study focused on green-synthesized magnetite/carbon dots nanocomposites (GS-Fe3O4/Cdots) utilizing Moringa oleifera leaf extract and watermelon peel waste for rapid degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye. Co-precipitation and hydrothermal method were used to synthesize GS-Fe3O4 and Cdots, respectively. In addition, the sonication method was used to link Cdots on Fe3O4 surface. X-ray diffraction spectrum of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots inform the cubic inverse spinel and has crystallite size in the range of 10.1–7.2 nm. The crystallite size also decreased with the increase of Cdots concentration. The transmission electron microscope showed the most uniform size of nanocomposites at around 13.4 nm. The functional group of Fe-O was detected on the nanocomposites, proof that Fe3O4 still exists after fabrication. The presence of C = C, C-O, and C-O-C also indicates the existence of Cdots on the surface of Fe3O4. The addition of Cdots affected the saturation magnetization and coercivity value in the range of 29.2 – 38.3 emu/g and 59–65 Oe, respectively, which showed a good magnetic properties. As an organic dye, MB was used for a photocatalytic process under UV irradiation. The degradation efficiency was raised to 98% for 30 min photocatalytic process. The magnetically separable capability makes nanocomposites could be recycled and reused three times with high degradation. Furthermore, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots potential as a low-cost and environmentally friendly reusable photocatalyst for rapid wastewater degradation.

XRD patterns of (a) GS-Fe3O4, (b) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 10 mL, (c) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 20 mL, and (d) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 30 mL nanoparticles.
(a) TEM images and (b) size distribution of Cdots.
(a) TEM images with corresponding SAED image and (b) size distribution of GS-Fe3O4, (c) TEM images with corresponding SAED image, and (d) size distribution of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 10 mL.
(a) EDX spectrum and (b) mapping analysis of element of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 10 mL, (c) EDX spectrum and (d) mapping analysis of element of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 30 mL.
FTIR spectrum of (a) Cdots, (b) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 10 mL, (c) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 20 mL, (d) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 30 mL, and (e) GS-Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

UV–vis absorption spectrum of (a) Cdots, (b) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 30 mL, (c) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 25 mL, (d) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 20 mL, (e) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 15 mL, (f) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 10 mL, and (g) GS-Fe3O4 nanoparticles.
Tauc plot analysis of (a) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots (Inset: Cdots), (b) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 10 mL, (c) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 15 mL, (d) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 20 mL, (e) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 25 mL, and (f) GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 30 mL.
Magnetic Hysteresis (M−H) curve of GS-Fe3O4 and GS-Fe3O4/Cdots with various concentration.
(a) Photocatalytic reduction efficiency of MB, (b) the effect of Cdots on photocatalytic reduction, and (c) the comparation of photocatalytic and control experiment.
(a) Pseudo-first order kinetic model and (b) rate constants of photocatalytic degradation.

(a) SEM-EDX spectrum, (b) Mapping analysis, and (c) bonding analysis of the photocatalyst after used.
Crystallite size, lattice parameter, and phase composition of GS-Fe3O4 and GS-Fe3O4/Cdots nanoparticles with various concentrations.
Band gap energies of Cdots, GS-Fe3O4, and GS-Fe3O4/Cdots with various concentrations.
Coercivity and magnetization saturation of GS-Fe3O4 and GS-Fe3O4/Cdots with various concentrations.
Comparison of Fe3O4/Cdots with various conventional method for wastewater degradation application.

row.entry: The MB degradation reached 80% in 30 min visible light exposure.
The photocatalytic result showed 89% degradation of MB after 40 min.
A removal efficiency of MB approximately 83% in 30 min. photocatalytic process under visible light.
The photocatalyst had a degradation efficiency of 50% for hexaconazole after 6th days in daylight condition.
Green synthesis route with physical attachment [This study]
GS-Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites with stable magnetic properties had degradation ability of MB reached 98% under 30 min UV exposure.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of magnetically separable and reusable Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites photocatalyst utilizing Moringa oleifera extract and watermelon peel for rapid dye degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study focused on green-synthesized magnetite/carbon dots nanocomposites (GS-Fe3O4/Cdots) utilizing Moringa oleifera leaf extract and watermelon peel waste for rapid degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye. Co-precipitation and hydrothermal method were used to synthesize GS-Fe3O4 and Cdots, respectively. In addition, the sonication method was used to link Cdots on Fe3O4 surface. X-ray diffraction spectrum of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots inform the cubic inverse spinel and has crystallite size in the range of 10.1–7.2 nm. The crystallite size also decreased with the increase of Cdots concentration. The transmission electron microscope showed the most uniform size of nanocomposites at around 13.4 nm. The functional group of Fe-O was detected on the nanocomposites, proof that Fe3O4 still exists after fabrication. The presence of C = C, C-O, and C-O-C also indicates the existence of Cdots on the surface of Fe3O4. The addition of Cdots affected the saturation magnetization and coercivity value in the range of 29.2 – 38.3 emu/g and 59–65 Oe, respectively, which showed a good magnetic properties. As an organic dye, MB was used for a photocatalytic process under UV irradiation. The degradation efficiency was raised to 98% for 30 min photocatalytic process. The magnetically separable capability makes nanocomposites could be recycled and reused three times with high degradation. Furthermore, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots potential as a low-cost and environmentally friendly reusable photocatalyst for rapid wastewater degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: Wastewater has become an important issue in this world. The biggest wastewater contributor is the textile industry, printing, dyeing, and other manufacturing [1–3]. In particular, wastewater from the textile industry contains organic dyes with complex aromatic molecules such as methylene blue (MB) that are non-biodegradable and hard to remove naturally, even if the dye concentration is less than 1 ppm in water. MB causes serious problems for natural environment, human health, and severe water scarcity if not treated properly [4,5]. Hence, wastewater treatment is needed.

Adsorption with activated carbon, filtration, photocatalysis, and various other techniques have been reported to treat wastewater [3,6]. Due to low-cost and efficient methods, photocatalysis has become prominent in wastewater treatment, especially MB removal [7,8]. The photocatalysis process utilizes UV-light to produce electron-hole pair in the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB), which can convert the organic wastewater into CO2 and H2O, so it not generated secondary pollutant and environmentally benign [4,9,10]. In photocatalysis process, the surface area is one of the parameters that play an important role in degradation [11]. According to the quantum size effect, nanomaterials have a larger surface area to exhibit more effective conversion ability. Therefore, using nanomaterial as a photocatalyst can be an alternative solution to improve photocatalysis performance.

Carbon nanodots (Cdots) are one of the nanomaterials that still attracting attention since discovered in 2004 [12]. Cdots are part of a zero-dimensional carbon family with an average size below 10 nm and are amorphous [13]. The most common ones of facile synthesis of Cdots are hydrothermal, chemical oxidation, laser ablation, and microwave [14,15]. Hydrothermal is the simplest carbonization process for Cdots preparation, that eco-friendly, affordable, and uniformed particle sizes can be obtained [16]. Natural and non-toxic carbon sources such as organic acids [4], fruit extracts [17], and food wastes, including banana peel [18] and watermelon peel waste [19], have been involved. In general, Cdots have unique and fascinating properties, such as excellent water solubility, non-toxicity characteristics, and edge effects leading to the detection of heavy metal ions [20] and CO2 reduction [21]. Due to strong photoresponsiveness (strong fluorescence and great absorbance), redox properties, and biocompatibility, Cdots offer an attractive alternative to photocatalysts [9,12]. Haryadi et al. utilized Cdots from biomass waste formed by simple carbonization for MB removal. MB was reduced to 40% within the 12 h photocatalysis process [22]. Das et al. reported a photocatalysis process using Cdots synthesized from pear juice via the hydrothermal method for 36 h at 180 °C that achieved ∼ 99.5% degradation of MB after 130 min [9].

However, due to the small size and lack of polarity of Cdots, it is difficult to remove or separate Cdots from the aqueous phase after the degradation process [4,23]. It is inefficient because the catalyst cannot be reused and requires a costly procedure to separate from the treated water. It is also not applicable to big-scale processes, especially industries. Therefore, the easy separation of the photocatalysts from the treated water and recycling it for repeated reuse is challenging. The promising solution for this problem, which is easy and inexpensive, is connecting Cdots with magnetic nanoparticles, for example magnetite (Fe3O4) [4,24]. Fe3O4 has outstanding biocompatibility, low toxicity, large surface area, high magnetization, and superparamagnetic performance [12,25]. The large surface area and the magnetic properties of Fe3O4 supports the water treatment process and allow effective separation from treated water with an external magnetic field, respectively [5,26,27]. However, combining Fe3O4 and Cdots makes nanocomposites accessible to controlled mobility and could be removed in short time by applied magnetic field after the photodegradation. In addition, nanocomposites could be recycled for the next reused and these features create effective, efficient, and increase the endurance of catalyst [26].

Previous studies have reported combining Fe3O4 with Cdots via reflux [28,29], stirring, sonication–called physical attachment [30,31], and hydrothermal method [24,32]. Fe3O4/Cdots were successfully formed after mixed Fe3O4 powder and n-propanol containing Cdots using sonication [31]. In other study, Fe3O4/Cdots were synthesized via hydrothermal method with temperature and reaction time variations for reusable photocatalyst [4]. Commercial Fe3O4 powder, acetic acid, and glucose are used as precursor in synthesized Fe3O4/Cdots which effectively remove organic pollutants via photocatalytic processes with efficient degradation reached 83% for 30 min. However, these previous studies used conventional methods to prepare nanoparticles. Conventional method has some disadvantages such as used chemical precursors, expensive hazardous reagents and produce harmful by-products for environment [33,34]. In order to minimize the drawbacks of conventional method, the use of chemical precursor should be reduce. Hence, this study offers a green-synthesis approach on prepared Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites.

Green synthesis has great advantages in term of environmentally friendly, energy-efficient, non-toxic, and cost-effective product that utilizing plant parts, such as leaves [35–37], fruit [38], peel [39], flower [40], or microorganisms [41]. Moringa oleifera (MO) leaf is one of the precursors often used in the green synthesis of nanoparticles. MO has an abundance of phytochemical substances, including phenolics and flavonoids, that play an important role as reducing agents and stabilizers in producing nanoparticles [42–44]. MO can cleanse the surface and precipitate organic or mineral particles in solution due to the cationic protein of coagulant properties [45]. We recently reported green-synthesized Fe3O4 utilizing MO leaf extract that has excellent magnetic properties [46–48]. Besides, the peel of fruits and waste as green-precursor of Cdots are also popular [49]. Although several studies have successfully produced Fe3O4 or Cdots based on green synthesis, the combining green synthesized Fe3O4/Cdots as nanocomposites for photodegradation is still lacking. The exploration of green-synthesized Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites photocatalysts for dye degradation is therefore essential and requires further studies. Herein, we studied for the first time, reusable Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites photocatalysts based on green synthesis route utilizing MO and watermelon peel for dye degradation.

This study develops green-synthesized Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites (GS-Fe3O4/Cdots) as magnetically separable and reusable photocatalysts for rapid degradation under UV irradiation. The structure, morphology, optical, and magnetic properties of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots were investigated to obtain the comprehensive study. The effect of the Cdots concentration on the degradation of MB was also investigated. Pseudo-first-order and coefficient of the degradation rate of photocatalytic activity was studied using Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model. Furthermore, the reusability of the green-synthesized nanocomposites was also observed in this research.

The chemicals precursor used in this study is Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), Ferrous sulphate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), ammonia (NH4OH) solution 25%, ethanol (CH3CH2OH) 96%, distilled water (DI water), and MB (C16H18N3SCI). All chemicals were purchased from Merck (Germany) and Sigma Aldrich (USA) that analytical grade without further purification. Furthermore, MO leaf extract and watermelon peel wastes were produced by Timurasa and the local market (Indonesia), respectively.

MO solution was obtained by heating MO extract powder in DI water with a ratio of 1:12 (w/v) at 60 °C for 1 h under thorough stirring. The mixture was cooled to room temperature and filtered using Whatman filter paper. MO solution was ready to use for further synthesis, and the stock solution could be kept in the refrigerator. This process can be seen in Fig. 1 (a).

GS-Fe3O4 was obtained by co-precipitation method, as reported before (see Fig. 1(b)) [48]. In brief, 4.054 g FeCl3·6H2O and 2.086 FeSO4·7H2O were dissolved in DI water and stirred for 15 min. MO solution was added to the mixture and continued stir at 60 °C. Then, 30 mL of 10% NH4OH solution was gradually added to the mixture under stirring for 90 min. The mixture color was changed from greenish-black to black, indicating the formation of Fe3O4. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting precipitate was separated from the solution using an external magnet and washed with DI water. The precipitates were dried at 100 °C for 2 h.

Fig. 1(c) shows a facile green synthesis of Cdots using watermelon peel waste via the hydrothermal method. Watermelon peel juice, ethanol 96%, and DI water with a ratio of (1:1:1) were stirred for 30 min. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. The reaction was kept at 180 °C for 3 h. Finally, the Cdots solution was filtered using filter paper Whatman and ready to use.

Fe3O4/Cdots were fabricated following the procedure (see Fig. 1(d)). Fe3O4 (0.8 g) was dissolved in 10 mL Cdots solution via sonication for 30 min and keep it in an isolated place for 24 h. Cdots can spontaneously anchor to the surface of Fe3O4 because favorable interaction encourages surface functional groups [31]. The mixture was magnetically separated and washed with DI water. Furthermore, the sample was dried at 90 °C for 4 h to obtain GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 10 mL. All steps above did to other variation of Cdots solution, there are 15 mL, 20 mL, 25 mL, and 30 mL, which represented GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 15 mL, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 20 mL, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 25 mL, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 30 mL.

The crystal structure and phase were evaluated by X-Ray diffraction (XRD) using Shimadzu XD-3H with Cu-kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) JEOL JEM-1400 observed morphology and size distribution. The surface morphology and the existence of Nanocomposites were investigated with Scanning Electron Microscope – Energy Dispersive X-Ray (SEM-EDX) using JSM-6510LA. The surface functional groups and magnetic properties were examined by Fourier transform Infra-red Spectrophotometer (FTIR) Prestige Shimadzu-21 and vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) Riken Denshi Co. Ltd., respectively. Optical properties such as absorbance and band gap energy were determined by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) Shimadzu Spectrophotometer UV-1900.

The photocatalytic process of MB was investigated by mixing 50 mg of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots with 50 mL of MB solution (7 ppm). The mixture was sonicated in the dark condition for 5 min to disperse the nanocomposites in a dye solution. A UV lamp (30 W) was the source to the MB photodegradation process for 1 h with constant stirring. After every 10 min irradiation, 5 mL of the reaction solution was removed and the photocatalyst was separated using a permanent magnet. The final solution was evaluated using UV–vis to determine the concentration of MB (λmax = 664 nm). The separated photocatalyst was washed three times to remove the remaining MB on the surface of nanocomposites and dried for 2 h at 100 °C to become powder. A reusability experiment was conducted with the same photocatalytic method using the recovered catalyst powder. A reusability experiment was labeled with R1, R2, and R3, referring to the first, second, and third.

Fig. 2 shows the XRD pattern of GS-Fe3O4 and GS-Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites with various concentrations analyzed by Rietveld refinement method using the MAUD program. The diffraction peaks of GS-Fe3O4 were observed at around 30.1°, 35.5°, 43.3°, 53.5°, 57.1°, and 62.8° correspond to the diffraction plane of (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440), respectively (Fig. 2(a)). According to the database (ICDD No.01-075-0033), the samples have a cubic inverse spinel structure of magnetite with a space group of Fd3m. Furthermore, the XRD spectrum of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 10 mL, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 20 mL, and GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 30 mL exhibit seven diffraction peaks that the six peaks are well agreed with the cubic inverse spinel structure of GS-Fe3O4 with the slightly shift (Fig. 2(b-d)). Another low-intensity peak was only detected on the spectrum of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites samples at around ∼23.9o , which could be attributed to the carbon of Cdots. This peak is close to graphite characteristic (002), even in a single phase, Cdots have a broad peak at around 23° as amorphous [4,20,50]. The low-intensity peak of carbon could represent a small quantity of Cdots in the nanocomposite. Moreover, the broadening and decreasing intensity of the main peak (311) of Fe3O4 on the diffraction of nanocomposites samples corresponds to the increasing Cdots concentration. It indicates the Cdots, which non-magnetic materials were formed on the surface of Fe3O4.

The broadening diffraction peak affected the crystallite size. The crystallite size was analyzed using Gaussian function of the main peak (311) and calculated using the Debye-Scherrer equation [51]. The estimated crystallite size of GS-Fe3O4 is 10.8 nm. In addition, the crystallite size decreased to 10.1, 8.9, and 7.2 nm for GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 10 mL, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 20 mL, and GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 30 mL, respectively (see Table 1 ). According to Bragg’s law, the lattice parameter was obtained at around 8.15 – 8.23 Å for GS-Fe3O4 and GS-Fe3O4/Cdots with various concentrations. These values are close to the standard data based on ICDD No.01–075-0033, which state the lattice parameters as 8.38 Å. The detailed lattice parameters is shown in Table 1. The phase composition of nanocomposites was obtained by the Rietveld refinement method using the MAUD program, which is shown in Table 1. The phase composition corresponds to the profile peak of Fe3O4 and carbon, and the increased Cdots concentration affected the broadening peak of Fe3O4. The addition of Cdots causes the change in lattice parameter of nanocomposites. Hence, it leads to crystal distortion on the interface and changed the strain value. Furthermore, the strain causes broadening peak diffraction and decreasing crystallite size [52]. It strengthened the state that Cdots are formed on the surface of Fe3O4.

The morphology and size distribution of Cdots are presented in Fig. 3 . The inset image in Fig. 3(a) shows the selected Cdots area. In Fig. 3(a), we can see that Cdots nanoparticles were monodisperse with uniform spherical morphology (indexed by the yellow line) [19,53,54]. The monodisperse property of Cdots makes it easier to react with other materials to form nanocomposites [55]. The spherical morphology of Cdots also increases the surface area. In addition, Cdots are well-dispersed in the solution that great potential to aqueous reaction, especially photocatalytic. The size of Cdots nanoparticles was analyzed using the normal distribution function from the TEM images. Fig. 3(b) shows Cdots diameter of 1.4 nm and narrow size distribution of 0.6 – 2.2 nm. Compare to other study, Cdots from watermelon peel waste produced via simple carbonization process (combustion at 220 °C for 2 h) has particle size 1.5 – 2.5 nm [19]. On the other hand, Cdots formation via hydrothermal process produce 2.6 nm with distribution 0.5 – 4 nm after 12 h reaction at 180 °C using leaves extract [56] and Das et al. also produce 3 – 4 nm after 36 h reaction at 180 °C using pear juice [9]. The size of the Cdots was smaller than other synthesized due to the different method and time reaction. In the hydrothermal method, Cdots were built from the extract of the precursor, called the growth process. The high temperature and longer time reaction affected the size of Cdots [57]. However, the smallest Cdots size has largest surface area that would be raise the performance.

Morphology of GS-Fe3O4 and GS-Fe3O4/Cdots with selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) and size distribution are shown in Fig. 4 . A semi-spherical shape was obtained for both samples. The diffraction rings of GS-Fe3O4 and GS-Fe3O4/Cdots on the SAED images are consistent with the hkl plane of XRD results and show polycrystalline of inverse spinel cubic. Although the nanoparticles clump together, GS-Fe3O4 has clear boundaries and easier to analyses the size using a normal distribution function. The average size of GS-Fe3O4 is close to 14.6 nm (Fig. 4(b)). Meanwhile, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots have no clear boundaries because of agglomeration. The agglomerated due to the van der Waals forces occur between nanoparticles because of the reactive surface area [51]. Moreover, Cdots had under 2 nm size related to the highly reactive surface area. Cdots may attach to the surface of Fe3O4, which changes the morphological environment and causes blurry images. The effect of nanoparticle size on surface reactivity is strengthened by the average size of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots is approximately 13.4 nm (Fig. 4(d)). The sharp bell-shaped distribution shows that the addition of Cdots makes the size of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites relatively uniform compared to GS-Fe3O4. The reduced size of nanocomposites is appropriate with the crystallite size from XRD analysis. In addition, this results in accordance with the previous studies that reported the Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites with various methods produce spherical shape with the average range size of 10 – 30 nm [4,24,28,30].

GS-Fe3O4/Cdots were characterized using the EDX to confirm the composition of chemical elements. Fig. 5 (a) shows the elemental composition of the GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 10 mL by the mass percent that contain Fe 58.9%, O 30.2%, and C 10.9%. Meanwhile the elemental composition of the GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 30 mL by the mass percent that contain Fe 63.5%, O 25.3%, and C 11.2% (Fig. 5(c)). Carbon was detected in two samples confirm that Cdots exist in the nanocomposites. Although there are slight differences in carbon percentage in the two samples, it can be proven that the concentration proportional to Cdots formation corresponds to their percentage. These results also strengthen the XRD and TEM result. Moreover, the Fe, O, and C elements were detected, confirming that Fe3O4/Cdots were successfully formed. Fig. 5(b) and (d) shows mapping image of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots elemental that indicated the lack of other impurities in the structure. The carbon elements are distributed around Fe3O4.

The FTIR spectrum of Cdots, GS-Fe3O4, and GS-Fe3O4/Cdots are shown in Fig. 6 , which confirms the presence of Cdots on the Fe3O4 surface based on molecular bond analysis. The GS-Fe3O4 can be characterized by the stretching vibration of Fe-O bond observed in the range of 579 – 632 cm−1 [58]. This stretching vibration was detected in all samples variation of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots, which had a relatively redshift. The shallow deep vibration spectrum at 1627 cm−1 on GS-Fe3O4 contribute to C = O, indicating the binding of biologically active compounds from MO, such as flavonoid [42]. However, the addition of Cdots on GS-Fe3O4 give other absorption peaks on the nanocomposites samples at around 1088, 1396, 1635, and 3426 cm−1 that could be indexed as C-O-C, C-O, C = C, and O-H bonds, respectively. These functional groups contribute to the characteristics of Cdots, especially the presence of C = C bond at 1641 cm−1 that indicate the core of Cdots [4,9]. Another functional group of Cdots is O-H bond at 3318 cm−1. Successfully combining materials into nanocomposites causes a change or creates a new bonding between materials, which show additional infrared absorption due to different energy vibration of bonding. This causes the wavenumber shift after combining two materials due to the interaction between Fe3O4 and Cdots [50].

Fig. 7 shows the absorbance spectra of the Cdots, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots, and GS-Fe3O4 were investigated using UV–visible spectroscopy. Cdots have strong absorption peaks at 282 nm attributed to the
π → π
* electronic transition of the aromatic C = C that corresponds to the FTIR result [57]. The peak provides information that Cdots from watermelon peel waste have strong absorbance in the UV range, so it has potential to be a photocatalyst under UV irradiation. On the other hand, GS-Fe3O4 and all variations of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots have similar absorbance profiles. Fe3O4 has a maximum absorption peak at around 193 nm and a broad shouldering characteristic peak in the range of 365 – 400 nm [10,59]. The existence of Cdots on the absorption spectrum of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites is not too obvious, but a small peak at around 212 – 223 nm can clarify the carbon still exists [57]. This peak is not only for GS-Fe3O4/Cdots, but also GS-Fe3O4 due to the utilizing MO, which contains a carbon chain of flavonoids. Although the peak is weak, it can be observed that increasing Cdots causes decreasing maximum peak of Fe3O4.

Furthermore, the absorption spectra were used to analyze the band gap energy (Eg ) using the Tauc plot equation [59]:

2 for indirect transition, 3/2 for a direct forbidden gap, and 1/2 for direct transition) [59]. Tauc plots analysis of the samples are shown in Fig. 8 . The Eg of Cdots, GS-Fe3O4, and GS-Fe3O4/Cdots with various concentrations can be observed by plotting

and extrapolating the linear part of the curve to the x-axis [60]. The Eg values are listed in Table 2 that show the increasing band gap energy of nanocomposites after addition of Cdots. According to electron confinement at nanoscale or called quantum size effect, the small particle size has high energy gap [61]. The overlapping of the energy level on nanoparticles less than the bulk that produce the narrower band width and causes an increase in energy gap between the VB and the CB. The higher energy gap will be restriction on the movement of electrons that means the nanoparticles tends to need higher energy to release electrons from the VB to the CB. The addition of Cdots can increase the band gap energy compared to GS-Fe3O4, in accordance with the decrease particle size by TEM and crystallite size results. The band gap energy of nanocomposites is also in accordance with UV irradiation energy. But decreasing the band gap energy of nanocomposites with highest Cdots concentration would be affected the photocatalytic process.

The magnetic properties are the main characteristics of separated photocatalyst potential. The M−H curves of GS-Fe3O4 and GS-Fe3O4/Cdots are presented in Fig. 9 . The external field was applied between ± 15 kOe at room temperature. The samples have small remanent magnetization and coercivity that show soft magnetic material with superparamagnetic behavior [4,48]. The saturation magnetization (Ms ) value of GS-Fe3O4 was 54.2 emu/g. Meanwhile, the Ms of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 10 mL was 38.3 emu/g and then decreased with increasing Cdots, as presented in Table 3 .

The presence of Cdots, non-magnetic materials on the surface of Fe3O4 inhibited the growth and movement magnetic moment of Fe3O4. It causes reduced magnetization due to reduced magnetic moment. The decreased Ms values indicate that the nanocomposites were successfully formed, corresponding to the other characterizations. The coercivity
(
H c
)
value of GS-Fe3O4 was 55 Oe and increased following increasing Cdots concentration up to 65 Oe for GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 30 mL (see Table 3). This is due to several factors such as non-magnetic atoms in the material, defect, surface effects, and strains [47,62]. Despite a decrease in Ms and an increase in Hc , the nanocomposites remained superparamagnetic so that the photocatalyst could be separated from the treated water after photocatalytic process.

The photocatalytic performance of the GS-Fe3O4/Cdots was evaluated for degradation of MB under UV irradiation for 1 h. MB degradation was conducted by observing the change of MB concentration on the solution. The characteristics absorbance of MB at 664 nm. According to the band gap analysis, Cdots have broad band gap energy, which affects the band gap of nanocomposites that can be excited by a high-energy photon. The high-energy photon of UV light leads to higher MB degradation than visible light. A combination of magnetic material and Cdots, generated electron-hole pairs in the conduction and valence bands. A hole can oxidize and produce hydroxyl radicals (·OH) that can break the long chemical chains of MB dyes [11]. Electrons can reduce oxygen and degrade the absorbed contaminants. The removal efficiency (D) was calculated using the following equation [10]:
(2)
D
%
=
C 0
-

× 100
where
C 0
represents the initial condition pollutants and
C t
is the concentration after time exposure.

Fig. 10 (a) represents the degradation activities of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots with various Cdots concentrations under UV light. In general, nanocomposites show the degradation ability of MB. The increasing Cdots on the GS-Fe3O4/Cdots trigger photocatalytic activities due to the degradation increase for a certain time (30 min.). Especially for the GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 25 mL that achieved high degradation. The degradation rate reached 97.6% and 98.1% after UV light exposure for 10 min and 30 min, respectively. The high degradation only in 10 min corresponds to the abundance of active site on the surface of nanocomposites. But degradation slightly decreased after 30 min exposure due to the saturation of nanocomposites. Moreover, the sample GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 30 mL produced lower degradation than GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 25 mL (see Fig. 10(b)). This is due to the band gap energy is no longer compatible with UV energy, so degradation is not optimal. This statement is consistent with the results of the calculated energy gap that there is a decrease in the band gap to 3.34 eV for nanocomposites. However, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 30 mL achieved 90.6% and 93.4% degradation of MB after 30 min and 60 min exposure, respectively. The degradation efficiency shows a great performance of catalyst employed. Fig. 10(c) shows the comparation between control experiment (photolysis and dark adsorption) and the photocatalytic using variation of nanocomposites on MB degradation. Photolysis process (without catalyst) achieved only 8% that indicate the natural process need a long time and can’t effectively degraded the MB. In addition, the nanocomposites have maximum adsorption until 35% removal and almost constant in 60 min that indicates the maximum adsorbent capacity. The powerful activity achieved with photocatalytic process with nanocomposites and UV irradiation as catalyst and light source, respectively. It proof that nanocomposites promote the degradation of MB.

Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) model for the photocatalytic activity of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots was evaluated to investigate the correlation of the enhanced photocatalytic activity. Fig. 11 (a) and (b) show the photocatalytic MB degradation was fitted using kinetic models and the rate constant of photocatalytic degradation, respectively. The experimental data were applied to the equation [62]:

) was calculated for 664 nm at 10 min photocatalytic process. The experiment data in accordance with L-H model. The rate constant value increases with the increasing Cdots concentration of 0.06 min−1, 0.08 min−1, 0.23 min−1, 0.37 min−1 for GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 10 mL, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 15 mL, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 20 mL, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 25 mL. The

a slight decrease to 0.17 for GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 30 mL causes a low energy gap explained before. However, it can be concluded that Cdots on the surface of magnetite affected photocatalytic activity, so that the GS-Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites can degrade MB to a simple molecule under UV irradiation.

The degradation efficiency can be explained by the basis of UV absorption capacity, active sites, and interfacial interaction on the photocatalyst. The UV light absorption promote the generation of electron-hole pairs (e–/h+ ). Cdots not only has great absorbance, but also higher photoluminescence with the lifetime of 7.6 ns [63]. Besides, Fe3O4 nanoparticle is weaker fluorescence due to good electrical conductivity that can inhibit recombination of e–/h+ . According to Zhu et al., nanocomposite would have lower lifetime such as for CdS/Fe3O4/g-C3N4 which have 1.5 ns [64]. The shorter lifetime on the nanocomposites induce faster interfacial e– transfer and greater separation of e–/h+ in the interface. Fe3O4 on the nanocomposites can facilitate to trap e– and support e–/h+ separation, which produce efficient charge carrier transfer for preferred photoreactions [9,64]. In addition, the surface of Cdots also can trap the e–
[9,63]. The high density of separated h+ lead the formation of active radicals, which makes major contribution to the degradation of MB.

The photocatalytic mechanism for GS-Fe3O4/Cdots under UV irradiation is shown in Fig. 12 . The UV light absorbed by GS-Fe3O4/Cdots lead exited electrons to produce e–/h+ . Most of this reaction occurs in Cdots that electron in VB exited to CB, leaving holes. In this case, photogenerated e–/h+ of GS-Fe3O4 can be ignored due to the lack of amount and efficiency [4]. Nevertheless, the superior electronic conductivity of Fe3+ in GS-Fe3O4 can promote the effective e–/h+ separation that reduce e–/h+ recombination by trap the e– species. Other photogenerated electrons in CB react with O2 on the surface of Cdots, which generate superoxide radicals (•O2 –). These superoxide radicals can reduction H2O2 produce hydroxyl radicals (•OH). The photogenerated hole in VB reacts with hydroxyl ion (OH–) and produces hydroxyl radicals (•OH). The radicals, especially hydroxyl radicals (•OH) can decompose MB into CO2 and H2O due to great oxidative ability. Furthermore, the specific surface area due to interaction between the dye and the catalyst is an important aspect of enhancing the photocatalytic activity.

The study of photocatalytic mechanism was improved with the radicals scavenger. The scavenger is added to determine the main active species or radicals which act in the photocatalytic system. The photocatalytic activity decreases after addition of a specific scavenger indicate the captured radical are the main active species in the photocatalytic system. In this study, several scavengers including isopropanol alcohol (IPA), AgNO3, and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) were used to the reaction solution. IPA, AgNO3, and EDTA as the scavenger of •OH, e– , and h+ , respectively. The photocatalytic process is conducted in 60 min using the highest powerful catalyst (GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 25 mL). The effect of the scavengers to the photocatalytic reaction can be seen in Fig. 13 . The blank label denotes a MB degradation without addition of scavenger that achieved highest degradation, 96.3%. The photocatalytic activity drastic decrease to 9.4% after the addition of IPA that capture •OH radicals. It shows that •OH radical play a key role in the degradation of MB. The addition of AgNO3 that captured e– affects the reduction of H2O2 to •OH is stopped. Therefore, the photocatalytic process is slower and achieved 73,1%. Besides, the addition of EDTA that capture the h+ causes the photocatalytic activity decrease to 17.5%. The h+ also has important role on the rapid •OH formation that has mention before. In this case, the main active species on degradation of MB using GS-Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites are •OH and h+ . This results strengthen the mechanism design.

The study of reusability of photocatalyst is conducted in this paper. After 60 min of one cycle photocatalytic process, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots were collected and separated from the aqueous phase using an applied magnetic field. Since the GS-Fe3O4/Cdots were conjugated together and attracted to applied magnetics, it evidences a recyclable photocatalyst application. In order to evaluate the reusability and stability for economic and high-performance catalysts, the dried recovered catalyst was re-dispersed into another new MB solution (50 mL) to initiate a second photodecomposing cycle using the same photocatalytic procedure. The recyclability of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots 25 mL was tested three times and the results are shown in Fig. 14 . The degradation efficiency decreases correspond to more cycles of the photocatalytic process. Fig. 14(a) shows that the first photocatalytic process achieved maximum efficiency up to 98.1% in just 30 min of exposure. In comparison, R1, R2, and R3 required an exposure time of 60 min to achieve maximum degradation efficiency. The decrease in degradation is related to mass loss after washing and drying. The mass of the photocatalyst is one of the essential parameters in the photocatalytic process. The mass reduction causes a reduction in surface activity and affects the next cycle of photodegradation. Although the degradation ability of nanocomposites was reduced by 47.4% after three cycles, the nanocomposites effectively degraded under UV irradiation up to 50.7% (Fig. 14(b)).

The recyclability of photocatalyst (GS-Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites 25 mL) is strengthened with the data recovery, as shown in Fig. 15 . The spectrum of SEM-EDX represents the elemental composition of the after used photocatalyst by the mass percent that contain Fe 55.6%, O 33.3%, and C 11.1% (Fig. 15(a)). The mapping image on the Fig. 15(b) also indicate the lack of other impurities in the structure of the after used photocatalyst. The existence of Fe, O, and C on the samples show the endurance of photocatalyst. In addition, the transmittance spectrum between before and after used photocatalyst shows the same profile (Fig. 15(c)). The bonding analysis also shows the consistent functional groups exist on the after used photocatalyst that confirm great reusable properties. Furthermore, this data recovery proof the decrease of degradation effiency on the more cycles due to the mass loss. Hence, this study points out that the GS-Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites have good recyclability and promising photocatalyst for environmental remediation, which has potential to be applied in industries.

Table 4 shows previous studies of combining Fe3O4/Cdots using conventional methods that formed toxic waste as a by-product. The different methods reported the same spherical morphology. Besides, the most of chemically synthesis such as hydrothermal and reflux need high temperature, consuming time, produce larger particle size and drastic decrease of Ms
[4,24,28]. Due to the easy route, relative small stable particle size and not change significantly Ms , the physical attachment was more suitable for combining Fe3O4/Cdots as photocatalyst [30].

The combination of Fe3O4 and Cdots via green synthesis encourages excellent photocatalytic activity. Using natural materials such as MO and watermelon peel promotes environmentally friendly features of photocatalyst. GS-Fe3O4/Cdots as photocatalysts had relatively stable magnetic properties that had the potential to recycle and reused for other photocatalytic processes.

The GS-Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites were successfully synthesized by combining Fe3O4 and Cdots utilizing MO leaves extract and watermelon peel waste, respectively, for rapid dye degradation. The structure, morphology, optical, and magnetic properties of nanocomposites were investigated. The existence of GS-Fe3O4/Cdots was proved by phase composition from XRD, SEM-EDX, and the binding from FTIR. The crystallite size GS-Fe3O4/Cdots was 10.1 nm with a cubic inverse spinel structure. Although saturation magnetization decrease corresponds to increasing Cdots, the nanocomposites still have strong magnetic properties. The large surface area and band gap energy are appropriate to MB degradation under UV irradiation. The GS-Fe3O4/Cdots achieved a degradation rate 98% for 30 min exposure and can be separated from the final degraded using an external magnetic field. The GS-Fe3O4/Cdots were also recycled for reuse three times with high degradation efficiency. Based on this study, GS-Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites are highly potential as an economical and effective reusable photocatalyst for future wastewater treatment and other environmental applications.
